title: JWT tokens should not be disclosed
description: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) are compact, URL-safe tokens used for authentication and authorization. When hardcoded in source code, they expose active session credentials that can be used to impersonate users, bypass authentication, and access protected resources for the duration of the token's validity.
recommendation: Remove hardcoded JWT tokens from source code. JWTs should be generated at runtime during the authentication flow and stored securely in memory or short-lived cookies. Invalidate any exposed tokens immediately.
impact: Exposed JWTs allow session hijacking, user impersonation, and unauthorized access to all resources the token's claims permit.

checks.message: JWT token with standard header detected (eyJhbGciOi prefix decodes to '{"alg":...'). This is a complete JSON Web Token containing a header, payload, and signature. Hardcoded JWTs expose active authentication sessions that can be replayed to impersonate the token's subject, access all APIs and resources the token is authorized for, and bypass authentication controls until the token expires. Invalidate this token immediately by rotating the signing secret or revoking the specific token in your token store.

Hardcoded JWT Bearer token detected in Authorization header. Bearer tokens in HTTP headers provide direct authenticated access to APIs. Hardcoding a JWT in an Authorization header exposes a complete session credential that attackers can extract and replay against the same API to gain full access as the token's subject. Remove from code and rotate the JWT signing secret immediately.

JWT token detected in variable assignment. Storing JWT tokens as hardcoded string literals in source code exposes live authentication credentials. JWTs encode user identity and permissions — attackers who obtain this token can make authenticated API requests as the token's subject until expiration. Rotate the JWT signing secret immediately to invalidate all tokens and implement proper token generation at runtime.
